desc: Prague is a highly walkable Central European capital of Gothic towers, baroque streetscapes, and vibrant beer-and-food culture. Visitor demand peaks in late spring, summer, and festive December periods, while shoulder months usually balance atmosphere and value more effectively.
hls: Compact center with easy tram access

todo.name: Prague Castle and St. Vitus
todo.desc: Tour courtyards, cathedral interiors, and Golden Lane for a deep look at Czech state history.

todo.name: Charles Bridge at dawn or dusk
todo.desc: Cross when crowds are thinner to enjoy statues, musicians, and river panoramas.

todo.name: Old Town Square and Astronomical Clock
todo.desc: Time your visit for the hourly clock show, then wander surrounding medieval lanes.

todo.name: Mala Strana and Lennon Wall walk
todo.desc: Explore baroque streets, embassies, and garden viewpoints on the castle-side bank.

todo.name: Czech beer hall evening
todo.desc: Try local lagers and hearty dishes in traditional pivnice settings.

todo.name: Petrin Hill or Letná viewpoints
todo.desc: Climb for broad city views and green-space breaks from the historic core.

wifi.note: Very strong and consistent in central accommodations, cafés, and coworking spaces.

prac.fltNote: Gatwick–Prague is commonly nonstop year-round with high frequency. Return fares are often ~£45–£120 off-peak, ~£120–£210 in shoulder demand, and ~£210+ around high summer and Christmas-market peaks.
prac.visa: Czechia is in Schengen; UK and US passport holders can generally stay visa-free up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
alerts: Historic-core crowding is heaviest late morning to evening in peak months.
Old Town accommodation can command large premiums versus tram-connected outer districts.
Seasonal events and Christmas markets tighten availability well in advance.

sweet: May–June and September are typically best for walkability and atmosphere with less crowd pressure than midsummer peaks.
